title: There's a planet made entirely of diamond — twice the size of Earth.
short: 55 Cancri e, about 40 light-years away, is believed to have a carbon-rich interior that formed into diamond under extreme pressure. Its estimated value: 26.9 nonillion dollars.
twist: The universe literally made a planet out of bling. We can't afford it.
more: 55 Cancri e orbits its star in just 18 hours and has surface temperatures around 2,400°C. Its carbon-rich composition, combined with the intense pressure of its interior, means a significant portion of the planet may be crystalline diamond. At roughly twice Earth's diameter and eight times its mass, the amount of diamond is staggering. Of course, mining it is slightly impractical — it's 40 light-years away and the surface is molten lava. But it's nice to know it's out there.

title: Crows can recognize your face — and hold grudges for years.
short: Researchers who captured and tagged crows wore specific masks. Years later, crows still scolded and dive-bombed anyone wearing those masks — even people the crows had never met before.
twist: They remember what you did. And they told their friends.
more: The University of Washington conducted a landmark study where researchers wore caveman masks while trapping crows. Years later, crows on campus still mobbed anyone wearing that specific mask — even new crows that weren't alive during the original capture. This means crows teach their offspring and peers which humans are dangerous. They have a social 'most wanted' list. Crows also bring gifts to humans who feed them regularly, use tools, solve multi-step puzzles, and have been observed holding what appear to be funerals — gathering silently around dead crows. Their intelligence rivals that of great apes.

title: There are more dead people on Facebook than living users in some countries.
short: By some estimates, over 30 million Facebook accounts belong to deceased users. The platform gains about 8,000 dead accounts per day. By 2070, dead users may outnumber living ones.
twist: The largest graveyard in history is a website.
more: A 2019 Oxford study projected that by 2070, Facebook's dead users could outnumber living ones — creating the world's largest digital graveyard. Facebook has introduced 'memorialized' accounts and 'legacy contacts' to manage this, but the philosophical questions are enormous. What happens to your digital identity after death? Who owns your posts, photos, and messages? Some researchers argue that social media is fundamentally changing how humanity processes grief — for the first time in history, the dead can still 'speak' through their archived posts.

title: The coldest temperature ever recorded in the universe was created on Earth.
short: Scientists at MIT cooled sodium atoms to 500 nanokelvin — half a billionth of a degree above absolute zero. That's colder than anything known to exist naturally in space.
twist: We out-froze the void of space. In a lab. On purpose.
more: The coldest natural temperature in the universe is the cosmic microwave background radiation at about 2.7 kelvin (-270.45°C). But in laboratories, scientists have achieved temperatures millions of times colder using laser cooling and magnetic traps. At these extreme temperatures, atoms behave in bizarre quantum ways — forming a state of matter called a Bose-Einstein condensate, where particles lose individual identity and act as a single quantum entity. This research isn't just academic curiosity — it's the foundation for quantum computing, ultra-precise atomic clocks, and sensors that could detect gravitational waves.

title: Crocodiles have barely changed in 200 million years. They watched the dinosaurs come and go.
short: Modern crocodilians are nearly identical to their Jurassic ancestors. They survived the asteroid that wiped out the dinosaurs, multiple ice ages, and continental shifts — without needing to evolve much.
twist: When your design is perfect, you don't need updates.
more: Crocodilians are sometimes called 'living fossils' — their body plan has remained virtually unchanged for over 200 million years. They survived the Cretaceous–Paleogene extinction event that killed 75% of all species, likely because of their ability to go months without food, their semi-aquatic lifestyle that provided shelter from environmental extremes, and their cold-blooded metabolism that requires far less energy than warm-blooded animals. They are, by any measure, one of evolution's greatest success stories.
